title: Understanding Literature
catalogDescription: Develops essential tools for close and informed reading of fiction, drama, and poetry. Considers how a text generates aesthetic pleasure, how it achieves moral or social impact. Develops skills in literary analysis through reading literary texts, through discussion, and through critical writing.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

title: Studies in American Literature
catalogDescription: Examines the aesthetic, social, and cultural expressions of American Literature through its major authors, modes, themes, and periods. Students will practice the analysis of literary discourse and the formation of critical arguments.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.label: UW Tacoma course descriptions (TLIT)
sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

notes: UW Tacoma catalog metadata parsed from the official UW course descriptions.

title: Literature and the Arts
catalogDescription: Examines the connections between literature and other art forms, such as film, painting, music, and performance. Emphasizes the methods of interpretation and critical theory in studying the relationships of artistic expression. Studies the work of major artists and writers, as well as examples at local galleries, museums, and performance spaces.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

title: Multi-Ethnic American Literature
catalogDescription: Examines multicultural and multi-ethnic literature by American authors. Focuses on novels, short stories, essays, and poetry that examine the social construction of race in American society, the construction of American identity, and the intersections of race, class, and gender.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

title: Introduction to Literature and Environment
catalogDescription: Examines the concepts of "nature", "environment", and "wilderness" across a range of literary texts produced by a variety of voices and considers how broader contexts-such as the historical, personal, or cultural-shape how writers represent nature and environment in their work.

title: Studies in English Literature
catalogDescription: Examines the aesthetic, social, and cultural expressions of English literature through its major authors, modes, themes, and periods. Students will practice the analysis of literary discourse and the formation of critical arguments.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

title: Ancient Literature of Western Civilization
catalogDescription: Examines works of literature and philosophy of ancient Western Civilization as the foundation for subsequent Western writing and thought. May include Homer's Odyssey, Sophocles' Oedipus the King, Plato's Apology, and Virgil's Aeneid.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.label: UW Tacoma course descriptions (TLIT)
sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

notes: UW Tacoma catalog metadata parsed from the official UW course descriptions.

title: Medieval and Renaissance Literature of Western Civilization
catalogDescription: Critically examines works of literature and literary theology from the medieval and Renaissance eras in Europe. Explores works as "archetypes," i.e., the foundation for subsequent European writing and thought of all kinds. Includes Dante s Inferno, Shakespeare's Hamlet, and Milton's Paradise Lost.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.label: UW Tacoma course descriptions (TLIT)
sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

notes: UW Tacoma catalog metadata parsed from the official UW course descriptions.

title: Modern Literature of Western Civilization
catalogDescription: Examines literary works of Western civilization from the modern era, works important to subsequent Western writing and thought of all kinds. May include Swift's Gulliver's Travels, Goethe's Faust, Kafka's short stories, and Woolf's Mrs. Dalloway.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.label: UW Tacoma course descriptions (TLIT)
sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

title: American Literary Movements, Genres, and Historical Periods
catalogDescription: Studies movements (Transcendentalism, Modernism, the Harlem Renaissance, etc.); genres (poetry, fiction, drama, essay); historical periods (American Renaissance, the ' 20s, etc.); and investigates the literature of ethnic, political, and/or regional groups. May be repeated for credit with instructor's approval.

sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

title: Studies in Selected American Writers
catalogDescription: Analysis of selected American writers, focusing on their depictions of success and failure, and their characteristic styles of affirmation and alienation. Are there typically American patterns that can be discerned? What makes a writer's vision compelling?

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.label: UW Tacoma course descriptions (TLIT)
sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

notes: UW Tacoma catalog metadata parsed from the official UW course descriptions.

title: Themes in American Literature
catalogDescription: Studies major themes addressed by writers in America. Includes topics such as: individualism, identity and community; sex, love and marriage; justice and injustice; industrialization, technology and the city; authenticity and egalitarianism; and race relations. May be repeated for credit with instructor's approval.

title: American Poetry
catalogDescription: An examination of different types of American poetry. Emphasizes writers from a variety of backgrounds. Poems approached from formal, thematic and historical perspectives.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

title: African American Literature from Slavery to the Present
catalogDescription: Readings, films, lectures, and class discussions will focus on constructions of racial identity, social consciousness, race class, and gender relations as reflected in novels, short stories, essays, and poetry by African American authors.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.label: UW Tacoma course descriptions (TLIT)
sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

notes: UW Tacoma catalog metadata parsed from the official UW course descriptions.

title: African American Women's Literature
catalogDescription: Examines female slave narratives and novels from the Harlem Renaissance, Social Protest Movement, and the contemporary period. Examines how black women illustrate social constructions and intersections of race, gender, and class. Readings, lectures, and films will explore the political motivation and public response to black women's writing.

title: Medical and Ethical Issues in Literature and Culture
catalogDescription: Examines various medical and bioethical issues through the lens of literature. Explores the role of technology, illness and culture, and end-of-live issues. Offered: jointly with T HLTH 325.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

title: Immigrant and Ethnic Literature
catalogDescription: Explores dynamics of cultures in contact and conflict and examines how literatures of different ethnic groups reflect this contrast. Emphasizes historical and cultural perspectives on immigrant and ethnic experience in the U.S. Analyzes literature depicting different aspects of the immigrant and ethnic experience within the larger context of America.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

notes: UW Tacoma catalog metadata parsed from the official UW course descriptions.

title: Asian American Literature
catalogDescription: Examines major works of Asian American literature and the "double burden" of Asian American writers in both creating art and representing a group. Compares this "burden" to those of writers of other ethnicities. Includes historic themes and represents voices of marginalized groups commenting on themselves and on mainstream society.

sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

title: Middle Eastern American Literature
catalogDescription: Focuses on literature produced by Middle Eastern Americans, examine how these texts explore questions of identity through intersections of race, gender, and class, as well as religious, historical, and sociopolitical contexts, and taking into consideration both popular culture and the traditions to which this literature responds.

title: Writing in the Pacific Northwest
catalogDescription: Examines the way place or region provides a context for writing. Compares several kinds of writing from the Pacific Northwest (e.g., history, journalism, fiction, nature writing) and analyzes how writing is made more effective by awareness of audience, setting and occasion for writing.

title: Shakespeare
catalogDescription: Examines selected works of English playwright William Shakespeare. Students read plays and engage in class discussion and textually supported interpretations in writing. Covers historical background of Shakespeare's England and play settings, as well as relevant theology, philosophy, and natural science.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

title: Ancient Greek Tragedy
catalogDescription: Examines selected tragedies of Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides.

title: Medieval Quests
catalogDescription: Examines important works of literature and literary theology from the medieval era, broadly construed. Special attention to the theme of the "quest." Texts include Augustine's Confessions, Beowulf, The Quest of the Holy Grail, Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, and Don Quixote.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

notes: UW Tacoma catalog metadata parsed from the official UW course descriptions.

title: The World Stage
catalogDescription: An investigation of western and non-western forms of staged performance from a historical, social, political, and cultural perspective. Subjects include the classical stage, medieval mystery plays, Jesuit theater, Noh and Kabuki theater, the Peking opera, Yiddish theater, Agit-Prop, the cabaret, the operatic gesamtkunstwerk, Avant-Garde theater, and Performance Art.

title: Myth and Literary Sagas in Creative Expression
catalogDescription: Explores the creative depictions of mythological themes, legendary heroes, and literary sagas. Special attention given to myth, legend, and literature in traditional cultural expression and their dramatic transformation in cinema. Themes include 'the hero of many faces,' and 'love and tragedy.'

title: Cross Cultural Studies in Contemporary Women's Fiction
catalogDescription: Examines novels and short stories concerned with race, politics, feminism, and the representation of women. Issues addressed include minority discourse, autobiographical modes, myth, storytelling, definitions of womanhood, and cultural identification. Writers studied include Allison, Erdrich, Silko, Kingston, Tan, Morrison, and Cisneros.

title: Varieties of Literary Criticism
catalogDescription: Investigates different approaches to reading and analysis of literary texts. Draws readings from a range of theoretical and practical criticism. Considers how critical theory adds to the understanding and enjoyment of literature. Gives attention to the history of critical ideas.

title: Science Fiction Literature
catalogDescription: Explores science fiction from various cultures and historical periods as a distinct literary genre that explores the boundaries, not just of space, but of moralities and belief systems, and what it means to be human; covering themes which may include robots/AI; alien encounters; speculative fiction; space voyaging, or time travel. Course overlaps with: ENGL 349. Recommended: Completed at least one 200-level literature course.
prerequisiteNotes: Official UW recommended-preparation text: Completed at least one 200-level literature

title: Children's and Young Adult Literature
catalogDescription: Explores the variety and richness of contemporary children's and young adult literature. Discusses current trends and issues, and explores multi-ethnic literature and literature from other countries.

title: Literature of the Harlem Renaissance
catalogDescription: Examines the images, themes, and characterizations in literature written by African Americans during the Harlem Renaissance. Writers include Johnson, Hughes, Larsen, Harston, Cullen, Fauset, Thurmann, White and McKay.

title: Contemporary Native American Women's Literature
catalogDescription: Examines novels, short stories, and poetry by contemporary Native American women authors. Addresses racial and gender oppression, reservation life, acculturation, political and social emergence as well as the leadership role of Native American women. Writers studied include Erdrich, Silko, Hogan, Tapahonso, and Harjo.

title: American Indian Literature
catalogDescription: Studies American Indian literature reflected in thematic and topical expressions. Examines content revolving around leading Native American writers, and/or non-Indian depictions of Native Americans, and/or American Indian biographical studies. May be repeated for credit with instructor's approval.

title: Native American Literature and Federal Indian Law (5) SSc/A&H, DIV
catalogDescription: Examines major Native American texts against the backdrop of federal Indian law. Native Americans have consistently been resisting and reacting to federal laws aimed at limiting their sovereign rights. Looks at how characters resist and undermine colonial forces in ways unique to Native American culture.

title: Topics in Literature and Environment
catalogDescription: Examines nature writing and environmental literature through the lens of a focused topic. Includes topics such as: Wilderness Tales, Nature and Industry, Women in Nature, Environmental Apocalypse, Nonhuman Animals in Literature, and Beyond Nature Writing. May be repeated for credit with instructor's approval.

title: Modern Novel
catalogDescription: Examines Cervantes' Don Quixote and twentieth-century works inspired by it. May include Don Quixote, Kafka's Castle, Borges' Labyrinths, and Nabokov's Pale Fire.

title: American Women's Literature: Nineteenth and Twentieth Century Texts
catalogDescription: Examines primarily novels and short stories by American women authors from the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Explores women's work, women's education, women's activism, marriage, motherhood, and crimes committed against and by women. Addresses the construction of female identity and how American women authors revise American history and literature.

title: Postcolonial Fiction
catalogDescription: Examines selected works of fiction by postcolonial authors while building a foundation in postcolonial history.

title: African Folklore and Literature
catalogDescription: Explores oral and written traditions in Africa. Emphasizes how the aesthetics of storytelling and dialogue shape the production of narrative in contemporary African contexts. Explores anthropological, literary, and historical approaches in viewing the aesthetic qualities of African folklore and literature.
